Page ii
How to Bid
Internet Absentee or Proxy Bidding
Place absentee bids now at www.StacksBowers.com or use our iOS or Android apps on your mobile device or 
tablet. Proxy bidding is enabled at midnight the day before the auction session starts live and is located on the 
lot description page. Visit our Frequently Asked Questions page at www.StacksBowers.com/FAQ/OnlineBid-
ding.aspx for more information. 
Stack’s Bowers Galleries iBid Live
Bid live anywhere in the world, on your computer at www.StacksBowers.com or use our iOS or Android apps 
on your mobile device or tablet.
Live Telephone Bidding
Phone Bidding must be arranged at least 24 hours before your session begins. Stack’s Bowers Galleries will ask 
for the lot numbers of interest and will call you during the auction so you can place bids with our representa-
tive in real time. Phone bidding is only offered for lots estimated at €2,500 or more. If you wish to arrange live 
bidding by phone, contact our Client Services Department at +45.80.40.49.42 or +1.949.253.0916 or email 
info@StacksBowers.com or infoDK@StacksBowers.com. 
Email and Mail Bidding
Use the enclosed bid sheet and email or mail it to us. Email bids to info@StacksBowers.com . If sending by 
mail, please allow sufficient time for the postal service. Please call to confirm that your bids have been re-
ceived by Stack’s Bowers Galleries.
Mail: A ttn. Client Services Department  Em ail: info@StacksBowers.com

 C osta Mesa, CA 92626  
 U nited States
Live Bidding
Contact our Client Services department at +45.80.40.49.42 (Copenhagen) or +1.949.253.0916 (United States)  
to pre-register for in-person bidding.

order the coins were cataloged in the 1928 L. E. Bruuns Mønt- og 
medaillesamling, resulting in some items appearing to be out of 
numismatic order due to more recent changes in attribution.

Page iv
Important Information About Bidding, 
Credit and Accepted Forms of Payment 
Below is a summary only. Please review the complete Terms and Conditions of Sale, beginning on page 323, 
or visit www.StacksBowers.com.
Stack’s Bowers Galleries iBid Live
Bid live anywhere in the world, on your computer at StacksBowers.com or use our iOS or Android apps on 
your mobile device or tablet.
Buyer’s Premium and Value Added Tax (V AT)
• For Buyers who take delivery of lots in non-EU countries, a premium of twenty percent (20%) based 
upon the total amount of the hammer (minimum of €25), will be added to all purchases of individual 
lots (the “Buyer’s Premium”). Danish V AT will not apply, provided that the lots in question are exported 
outside of the EU within 30 days from the date of the Auction Sale.
• Buyers which are non-V AT registered entities or individuals who take delivery of lots within EU 
countries or Monaco are subject to margin scheme taxation (in Danish “brugtmoms”) and will be in -
voiced a V AT charge and a Buyer’s Premium for a total of twenty five percent (25%) based upon the total 
amount of the hammer (minimum of €25), which will be added to all purchases of individual lots. For 
Buyers who take delivery of lots within EU countries which are either V AT-exempt or subject to regular 
V AT, a Buyer’s Premium of twenty percent (20%) based upon the total amount of the hammer (minimum 
of €25), will be added (excluding Danish V AT on the hammer plus Buyer’s Premium). 
• Institutional EU Buyers with a valid V AT number registered in an EU member state (other than Den-
mark) will be charged a Buyer’s Premium, but may be exempt from Danish V AT if said EU Buyer pro-
vides the requisite statutory registration documents and can provide documentation that the lot has been 
transported to the foreign address (i.e. outside Denmark) provided by the EU Buyer to the Auctioneer. 
These documents comprise the V AT registration number and the acknowledgement of receipt from the 
territory of the EU member state in which the V AT registration was issued. Should the Auctioneer effect 
the export of any lots, and provided a valid V AT registration document was submitted to the Auctioneer 
before purchase, V AT will not be charged.
Summary of Buyer’s Premium and Value Added Tax (V AT)
*For lots sold under the margin scheme*

========== PAGE 7 ==========

 Pa ge v
Taxable persons are registered for V AT and acquire coins with reference to this registration, e.g. resellers.
Non-taxable persons do not acquire coins with reference to a V AT registration, e.g. collectors.
Lots subject to regular V AT will be marked on the website and in the catalog with the symbol “u” or with the 
text “This item is subject to full V AT” .
Lots which are not subject to V AT will be marked on the website and in the catalog with the symbol “s” or 
with the text “This item is V AT free” .
Placing Bids 
• All bids must be made in Euros. 
• All invoices will be made in Euros.
Bid Limits 
• Bidders without pre-approval by Stack’s Bowers Galleries that plan to bid in excess of their bid limit must 
contact Stack’s Bowers Galleries in advance. A 25% deposit will be required at least 48 hours before the 
start of the auction paid by cashier’s check or money order, approved check, wire transfer or other im-
mediate bank transfer. Any unused deposit will be promptly returned to the bidder. 
Shipping
• All lots will be shipped from Denmark.  Any import (and/or turnover) tax and duty payable abroad is on 
Buyer’s account in every case.
Questions? 
• If you have any questions about bidding, credit or payments, please contact us at 
+1.949.253.0916 / +45.80.40.49.42 or e-mail info@StacksBowers.com / infoDK@StacksBowers.com. 
Accepted Forms of Payment 
• We welcome payment by wire transfer or other immediate bank transfer in Euros.   
• Cash payments ARE NOT accepted. 
• Payment via credit card will not be accepted without prior approval of Stack’s Bowers Galleries.  
All payments by credit card will incur a surcharge of 2.9%. 
• All invoices will be made in Euros. 
Bank Wire Information
Always call to verbally confirm wire information to prevent fraud.

Page viii
Page viii


========== PAGE 11 ==========

 P age ix
 P age ix
It is hard to believe that less than three years ago, I was huddled around a 
small table in a vault with several colleagues examining tray after tray of 
coins from the Bruun Collection for the first time. It was impossible to at-
tain a detailed grasp of 20,000 objects in that short amount of time, but the 
team was so excited and overwhelmed by what we saw that we immediately 
bounced around ideas of how to best market each segment of the collection. How do you best market 
a collection that touches upon 1,000 years of Scandinavian history and includes a broad geographic 
reach spanning northern Europe to the Danish colonies in the Caribbean and India?
Well that theoretical question became real once we had been chosen to present the Bruun Collection. 
Early on we began to plan an auction in New Y ork, with an eye toward presenting in that location not 
only some of the colonial coins—given that the Danish West Indies was sold to the U.S. in 1917 and is 
now known as the United States Virgin Islands—but also to bring some of the best quality and highest 
grade coins to the U.S. market. In the last few decades, influenced by third-party grading companies 
and a large supply of high-grade 19th and 20th century U.S. coinage, American tastes have taken a 
sharp turn toward superb quality coins, whether they be the finest known examples of major rarities 
or simply among the best preserved examples of very common coins. 
The result of our years of thoughtful planning to create a sale with an American bent is the catalog and 
auction in your hands. Commencing with startlingly high grade coins of Danish king Cnut of England 
and wending its way through time and geography to the early 20th century coins of Scandinavia, the 
auction’s content has been hand selected for its rarity, beauty, or superb quality. Some of the 19th cen-
tury and early 20th century Danish, Norwegian, and Swedish coinages are essentially perfect, prob-
ably having come straight from the mints into Bruun’s hands, or acquired by a prior collector directly 
from the mints and passing to Bruun through private purchase. They boast mark-free surfaces and 
just light and pleasant toning from a lifetime spent in a cabinet rather than in commerce.  There are 
some exquisite quality medals offered as well, highlighted by a series of lightly toned, gleaming Superb 
Gem silver restrikes of Scandinavian medals, some probably commissioned by Bruun himself. Their 
quality and appearance are, quite simply, mesmerizing. 
Also mesmerizing is the massive Akershus 10 Ducats or Portugaloser of 1661. It is, according to our 
cataloger, “arguably the most famous, beautiful, and coveted of all Norwegian gold coins. ” It is also 
unique, meaning that only one person in the world can possess an example of this superb “city view” 
type. 
Though not everyone will be able to possess the Akershus Portugaloser, we encourage all who can to 
come to New Y ork City to visit with this coin and the hundreds of others in this auction. Once they are 
dispersed to the four winds, seeing this many amazing Scandinavian coins in one place will require a 
special trip to the national collections of Denmark, Norway and Sweden. While you are in New Y ork, 
be sure to also come visit us in our gallery at 470 Park Ave, just a short walk from the lot exhibition 
and auction at the Kosciuszko Foundation. We would love to have you! 
Vicken Y egparian
Executive Vice President, Stack’s Bowers Galleries
Welcome Back to the L. E. B ruun Collection: 
A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History
Part IV


========== PAGE 12 ==========

Page x
Preface
“Start spreadin’ the news!”  With the fourth installment of the auction of L. E. 
Bruun’s legendary coin collection, Stack’s Bowers Galleries is headed to New Y ork 
- and I couldn’t be more excited. From the very first auction, our American cli-
ents have proven they recognize that a collection of this caliber has never been, 
and will never be, offered again. Virtually every record for Danish and Norwe -
gian coins has been shattered; the rarity and quality we’ve seen are simply beyond 
anything previously encountered.
For years, the majority of American collectors have embraced the technical precision of the Sheldon 
Scale. They understand how exceptionally high-graded examples of otherwise common minor coins - 
besides being aesthetically stunning - can be so extremely rare that they command hammer prices many 
times higher than the standard for the type. As I noted in my preface to Catalog III, labeling rare Medi-
eval or Renaissance coins as ‘TOP POP’ doesn’t always make sense, as they may be the only specimens 
ever graded. However, for coins from the 19th century onwards, it’s a completely different story. It has 
been a particular pleasure to mark almost every coin from this period in the current catalog as ‘TOP 
POP , ’ fully confident that new records are about to be set.
In this catalog, we are proud to present - for the first time - coins from the Danish colonies in India and 
the West Indies, which have always drawn significant interest from American collectors. Here, too, the 
quality and rarity reach extraordinary heights.
Another new addition is the selection of historical medals, led by the world’s largest Baroque medal, 
struck to commemorate the Danish victory over the Swedes at the Battle of Køge Bay in 1677. This is fol-
lowed by Gianelli’s 1792 medal marking the abolition of the slave trade and an exceptional gold bravery 
medal from the Battle of Copenhagen in 1801. Compared to the coins, the echoes of history feel even 
more profound here.
For those who appreciate coins casting an unusually long shadow, the numerous double, triple, and even 
quadruple Norwegian Speciedalers are worth noting—several of which are unique in private hands. Oh, 
I almost forgot to mention arguably the single most desirable Norwegian gold coin in existence, the 
Frederik III Akershus Portugaloser:
When Lars Emil Bruun traveled to the U.S. in 1920, it was partly to promote his company, The Danish 
Butter Export Co., and partly to meet the famous American collector Virgil Michael Brand. Bruun was 
unimpressed; he found Brand somewhat uncouth and unsystematic, lacking insight into his own hold-
ings and collecting for volume and value alone. This stood in stark contrast to Bruun’s own sharp vision: 
to document Scandinavian numismatic history from beginning to end.
It is my great hope that not only American collectors, but collectors worldwide, will sense Bruun’s mag-
nificent vision within this catalog and choose to become part of the story of one of the world’s most 
extraordinary coin collections, and enter the auction room singing: “If I can make it here….. ”
Michael Fornitz
Director of Stack’s Bowers Galleries Denmark


========== PAGE 13 ==========

 P age xi
Forord
“Start spreadin’ the news!” Med den fjerde del af auktionen over L. E. Bruuns leg-
endariske møntsamling sætter Stack’s Bowers Galleries kurs mod New Y ork, og 
det har jeg glædet mig til! Allerede fra den første auktion har vores amerikanske 
kunder vist, at de forstod, at en samling af tilsvarende kaliber aldrig før har været 
set og aldrig vil blive udbudt igen. Når både sjældenhed og kvalitet er af højeste 
kaliber, kan det derfor heller ikke overraske, at stort set alle rekorder for danske 
og norske mønter har stået for fald. 
I årevis har hovedparten af de amerikanske samlere sværget til den tekniske og meget findelte kvalitets-
gradering kaldet Sheldon-skalaen. De har indset, hvordan meget højt graderede eksemplarer af ellers al-
mindelige småmønter, der, udover – i sagens natur – at være ualmindeligt æstetisk tiltalende, kan være så 
ekstremt sjældne, at de bør belønnes med et hammerslag, der ligger mange gange over, hvad der normalt 
betales for en tilsvarende type. Som jeg nævnte i mit forord til Katalog III, giver det ikke nødvendigvis 
megen mening at betegne sjældne mønter fra middelalderen eller renæssancen for TOP POP , da det ofte 
kan være de eneste eksemplarer, der nogensinde er blevet graderet. Når det drejer sig om mønter fra lidt 
ind i det 19. århundrede og frem, er det imidlertid en helt anden snak. Det har derfor været en ganske 
særlig fornøjelse at markere stort set samtlige mønter fra denne periode i nuværende katalog som TOP 
POP , i sikker forvisning om at nye rekorder vil blive sat.
I dette katalog præsenterer vi for første gang mønter fra de danske kolonier i Indien og Vestindien, som 
altid har nydt særlig amerikansk bevågenhed. Også her når både kvalitet og sjældenhed ekstraordinære 
højder. 
En anden nyhed er udbuddet af historiske medaljer, anført af verdens største barokmedalje, præget i 
anledning af den danske sejr over svenskerne i Slaget i Køge Bugt i 1677, fulgt op af Gianelli’s medalje fra 
1792, der markerer ophævelsen af slavehandlen, og en exceptionel tapperhedsmedalje i guld fra Slaget på 
Reden i 1801. Her lyder historiens vingeslag som mere end blot en susen!
Holder man af mønter, der kaster en usædvanlig lang skygge, er det værd at bemærke de mange dobbelte, 
tredobbelte og endog firdobbelte norske speciedalere, hvoraf de fleste er unikke i privat eje. Og mon ikke 
jeg i forbifarten bør nævne det, der vel nok må betegnes som den mest eftertragtede norske guldmønt 
overhovedet: Frederik III’s Akershus Portugaløser.
Da Lars Emil Bruun rejste til USA i 1920, var det dels for at promovere sit firma, The Danish Butter 
Export Co., og dels for at møde den berømte amerikanske samler Virgil Michael Brand. Bruun var ikke 
imponeret; han fandt Brand både “bondsk” og usystematisk, uden dybere indsigt i sin egen samling og 
med fokus udelukkende på volumen og værdi. Dette stod i skarp kontrast til Bruun, der havde en præcis 
vision om at dokumentere den skandinaviske mønthistorie fra start til slut. 
Det er mit store håb, at ikke blot amerikanske samlere, men samlere i hele verden i dette katalog vil 
kunne fornemme Bruuns storslåede vision, vælge selv at blive en del af historien om en af verdens mest 
ekstraordinære møntsamlinger, og deltage i auktionen, nynnende: “If I can make it here.... ”
Michael Fornitz
Direktør for Stack’s Bowers Galleries Danmark


========== PAGE 14 ==========

Page xii
Wholesale price list from 1896 of L. E. B ruun’s canned Danish Butter, targeting the British market.


========== PAGE 15 ==========

 P age xiii
The Evolution of the L. E. B ruun Collection
Many of you have undoubtedly encountered some version of the joke “The best way to make a small 
fortune in the stock market is to start with a large one. ” Well if we can call L. E. Bruun’s current 20,000 
piece collection of coins, medals and banknotes “small, ” then we can alter the joke to “The best way 
to assemble a small collection of coins is to start with a large one. ”  And in some ways, this is what  
L. E. Bruun did over his nearly 65 years of collecting. However, rather than cleaving off large sections 
of a gargantuan collection, the trajectory of Bruun’s collection as it stands now was a much more 
curated and organic process. 
In an interview on the occasion of his 70th birthday published in the 
March 17, 1922 issue of Danish weekly Hver 8 Dag, Bruun related that 
the origins of his collection extend back to 1859, when an uncle had 
passed away, leaving some coins that were distributed among the chil-
dren in the family. Bruun was about seven years old at the time, an age 
when many children collect something as a means of learning about 
their environment. For most, this is a passing phase in childhood de-
velopment. For a precious few, it is just the beginning, with collect -
ing becoming a lifelong “unruly passion, ” as one author has styled it. 
Bruun was among those precious few. 
Even as a young apprentice in Holbӕk with no disposable income, he 
managed to collect coins, documenting 49 silver coins and 87 coppers 
from around the world in an 1867 listing of his collection, when he 
was just 15 years old. His collecting continued and blossomed in the 
1880s when his butter wholesale and export business took off; this became the inflection point in his 
financial life that provided the resources to buy not only individual coins but even entire collections 
of them. 
He originally and naturally focused on the coins of Denmark and Norway, quickly building up what 
he considered the largest collections in these fields, buying both privately and at public auctions such 
as the sales of the Alfred Benzon collection in Copenhagen in the 1880s. A more complete view of 
the sources, both public and private, for the Scandinavian coins in the Bruun Collection may be seen 
in the list of “Important Auctions and Private Purchases” , provided as an appendix to this auction 
catalog. The listing is a veritable who’s who of Scandinavian numismatics during not only Bruun’s 
lifetime but also the century before. 
He added Swe dish coins to the mix and then English coins in the 1890s, buying at many of the 
famous name sales in England: Montagu, Murdoch, Hilton, Price, Rashleigh, Roth, Walters, and 
Carlyon-Britton. 
The butter business, along with his shrewd investments in real estate on the outskirts of Copen -
hagen, provided the means to collect coins at a high level. The coins provided an escape from the 
day-to-day of the business world, as he related in the Hver 8 Dag interview: “The good thing about 
collecting coins is that when you are upset about something or you feel unsettled, then you go and 
look at your coins, and then you calm down by studying them again and again pondering the many 
unsolved problems they present. People who are exclusively devoted to their business make a great 
mistake. I, for one, could never imagine thinking about nothing but butter until my dying days. ”
His rise to prominence in the field of Danish numismatics landed him an invitation to become a 
founding member of the Danish Numismatic Society in 1885, which undoubtedly provided op -
portunities to rub shoulders with the prominent Danish numismatists of the day and acquire coins 
privately. The pace and extent of his collecting must have been such that he felt the need to sell and 
trade coins during his lifetime, and he is known to have traded items privately within the numismat-
ic milieu of fin-de-siècle Copenhagen. He also auctioned off more than 1,000 lots of coins through 
L. E. Bruun in 1922, around 
the time of his 70th birthday. 

========== PAGE 16 ==========

Page xiv
a general auction in 1894, a named single-owner sale in 1898, and an anonymous single-owner sale 
in 1901.
He was convinced to start auctioning off his Swedish collection, which he felt was the best in pri-
vate hands, a decision he came to regret. The first auction was through Adolph Hess in Frankfurt 
in May 1914, and a second auction was planned for October 1914, but those plans were scuttled by 
the outbreak of World War I. The coins intended for the second auction were instead sold to Swed-
ish merchant and big-time collector Israël Berghman (1864-1945), who sold off a few duplicates at 
auction in 1918. Berghman sold the remainder of the Bruun collection en bloc to Amsterdam dealer 
Jacques Schulman, who in turn resold the collection intact to famed American beer brewer and col-
lector extraordinaire Virgil Brand. 
Bruun’s two biggest coups came late in his life. In 1918, he purchased 
the entire coin collection of J.G. Guildal, who had himself swallowed up 
the entire collection of Major General Carl Thorvald Jørgensen (1819-
1902) in 1901. Bruun’s crowning achievement, though, was the 1922 
acquisition of the multigenerational Countship of Brahesminde Collec-
tion, built by the Bille-Brahe clan commencing circa 1800. Additional 
details on these important collectors and collections can be found in 
this catalog on page 93 (Guildal) and page 31 (Brahesminde). 
Bruun’s collections were already quite advanced at the time of these two 
important acquisitions, which resulted in a fair amount of duplication 
in the resulting blended collection. Bruun himself sold off some of the 
Guildal duplicates to prominent Danish collectors, but it wasn’t until af-
ter his passing that the Bille-Brahe collection was fully integrated into 
Bruun’s. The duplicates, selected by Danish numismatists Georg Galster 
and Hans Henrik Schou, were sold at auction under the auspices of Hol-
ger Hede in a 6,241 lot auction in October 1925. Also in 1925 were a pair 
of auctions by Sotheby’s in London that dispersed his British, Continental and American collec-
tions, while over 500 medieval pennies of Northumberland, Ireland, as well as Nordic imitations of 
Aethelred’s coins were donated to the Danish Royal Collection of Coins and Medals by a provision 
of Bruun’s will.
Given its depth and size, the Bille-Brahe collection was long considered a reserve for the Danish 
National Collection, a mantle that passed to Bruun with the acquisition of the Bille-Brahe cabinet. 
As a patriotic Dane and the most prominent collector of his day, Bruun took seriously the idea that 
his collection was now an informal reserve for the Danish National Collection. Though Copenhagen 
and all Denmark were spared the destruction suffered by much of Europe during World War I, he 
was keenly aware that Copenhagen had been mostly destroyed by British bombardment in 1807, and 
that a future world conflagration could result in the loss or destruction of the National Collection. 
In 1922 he amended his will to formalize the concept of his collection acting as a reserve for the Na-
tional Collection: “For a period of 100 years after my death, the collection shall serve as a reserve for 
the Royal Coin and Medal Collection in Copenhagen in the sense that if it should be substantially 
destroyed by fire or lost by theft or other such event, my collection shall be offered to the State as a 
gift. ” However, should the next century pass with the national collection intact, “it shall be sold at 
public auction and the proceeds shall accrue to the persons who are my direct descendants. ”
On November 21, 2023, exactly 100 years after Bruun’s death, the waiting period ran out. With the 
Royal Danish Coin and Medal Collection intact, L. E. Bruun’s fantastic 20,000-piece collection of 
Danish, Norwegian and Swedish numismatics built and curated through decades of acquisitions and 
conscious whittling, became destined for the auction block. The fruits of nearly 200 years of labor of 
generations of collector-curators, culminating with L. E. Bruun, will re-enter the numismatic mar-
ketplace through a series of Stack’s Bowers Galleries auctions over the coming years.
The catalog of the  
L. E. Bruun Collection  
duplicates sold at auction 
in Copenhagen in  
October 1925.

========== PAGE 20 ==========

Page 4 Stack’s Bowers Galleries
The L.  E. Bruun Co llection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part IV
COINS OF DENMARK
Iron Age Gold Bracteate of Great Beauty and Historical Interest
17013
DENMARK. Gold Bracteate, ND (500-550). Northern Jutland (?). Migration Period. Mackeprang-340; Bruun-1180 
(illustrated page 47). Weight: 1.29 gms. Suspension loop removed. A daylight crack is noted, but both the impression 
and the overall condition is exquisite.
Since the foundation of numismatics as a proper science in Denmark, the Scandinavian gold bracteates of the Migration 
Period have been treated alongside coins (cf. e.g., Bartholin, Antiquities Danicæ, 1689; Museum Regium, 1696 and 1710; 
Beskrivelsen af 1791, Class I, Plate I). Historically, many of the most prominent collectors took pride in being able to begin 
their collections of Danish “coins” in this distinguished manner (including Timm, Thomsen, Bruun, Hauberg and Glück-
stadt). As all bracteates without a very long pedigree are however now considered National treasure, it is very rare to find 
these in the market today.
The motif on this type (academically referred to as type D) is highly stylized and - like here - most often depicts contorted 
mythical animals (Salin’s Style I). This animal style is characteristic of the 6th century and differs from the more anthropo-
morphic motifs found on the older types. 
€15,000-€20,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).
Ex: Georg F. Timm Collection (Copenhagen - 1831) Lot # 2. (Original collector’s ticket included)

========== PAGE 21 ==========

 Bid Online at www.StacksBowers.com Pa ge 5
Session I • Tuesday , March 24, 2026 • 12:00  PM ET / 5:00  PM CET
    
  
Photo Enlarged 2x
17014
DENMARK. Semi-Bracteate, ND (965-75). Harald Blåtand 
(Bluetooth). NGC MS-63. Hauberg-1; Malmer-KG 9a (C1b, Pl. 
10:18 / C1a, Pl. 21:10); Bruun-1207; Weight: 0.33 gms. Perfectly 
struck and with a seductive bluish patina this Choice exemplar 
is bound to attract considerable attention. No other examples of 
this interesting variant with an extra cross on the lower right on 
the “Dorestat-side” was known to Brita Malmer.
Harald Bluetooth, who is normally credited with being the first 
Danish king to adopt Christianity has incidentally given name to 
the short-range wireless program known as “Bluetooth”. 
€1,200-€1,600
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
    
  
Photo Enlarged 2x
17015
DENMARK. Semi-Bracetate, ND (958-85). Haithabu (Hede -
by) Mint. Harald Blåtand (Bluetooth). NGC MS-62. Hau -
berg-2; Malmer-KG 10a (A3e, Pl.29:8 / A2a, Pl.31:14); Bru -
un-1290 (illustrated plate 2); Weight: 0.29 gms. Tiny daylight 
strike-through. A truly exceptional near Choice Mint State sur-
vivor of a very interesting variant of which Brita Malmer had 
only recorded the present piece. 
€2,000-€3,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
    
  
Photo Enlarged 2x
17016
DENMARK. Penny, ND (1035-42). Lund Mint. Harthac -
nut (Hardeknud). NGC MS-62. Hauberg-9; Hauberg Collec-
tion-509 (electroplate copy); C. J. Becker-H 13/63; Bruun-1398; 
Weight: 0.83 gms. Moneyer: Carle. A spectacular piece with su-
perb details and an exceptional luster. Becker knew of only three 
examples, of which this is the only one in private ownership. The 
other two are in the Danish National Museum (defect) and in 
Lund.
Although the Short Cross type was completely dominant in Eng -
land during the last years of Cnut (1029-36) and the earliest reign 
of Harthacnut, it never became a major type in Denmark. 
€2,000-€3,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
    
  
Photo Enlarged 2x
17017
DENMARK. Penny, ND (1044-46). Lund Mint. Civil War 
(stridsperioden). NGC MS-63. Hauberg-23 (Harthacnut); 
Hauberg Collection-683 (Magnus); C. J. Becker-MX 25/77; Bru-
un-1419; Weight: 1.05 gms. Moneyer: “Emcr” . Choice mintstate 
exemplar sharply struck on a completely even flan and with an 
utterly irresistible turquoise patina.
Struck during the troubled years of fighting for power between the 
Norwegian king Magnus and Sven, the nephew of Cnut the Great. 
€1,500-€2,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
GEORG FRIEDERICH TIMM  
(1746-1829)  
Georg Friederich Timm (1746-1829) worked as a metalworker at the 
royal court and had a highly interesting collection of coins and med-
als, including many rarities. The catalog from his estate auction in 
1831 was written by two of the most prominent numismatists of the 
time, Ole Devegge and Christian Jürgensen Thomsen, and is still of 
considerable scientific value.


========== PAGE 22 ==========

Page 6 Stack’s Bowers Galleries
The L.  E. Bruun Co llection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part IV

17018

den Store). NGC MS-64. Hauberg-54; Hauberg Collection-80; 
Bruun-1515 (Harthacnut). Weight: 0.73 gms. Moneyer: Un -
known (blundered legends). A nothing less than fabulous Near 
Gem of this interesting type with a an almost surrealistically dis-
soluted portrait facing right.

From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

Pennies struck for Olaf the Holy, c. 1025 (NMH-4-5)

DENMARK. Penny, ND (1035-42). Haithabu (Hedeby) Mint.

Collection-616; Bruun-1538 (illustrated plate 5). Weight: 0.80 
gms. Moneyer: Unknown (blundered legends).

condition, it is also struck on an unusually heavy planchet and 
from a pair of dies which were cut with much more skill and 
precision that normally found on this type.

From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
    
  
Photo Enlarged 2x
17021
DENMARK. Penny, ND (1042-47). Lund. Magnus I, “the 
Good” (Magnus den gode). NGC MS-62. Hauberg-6; Hauberg 
Collection-685; C. J. Becker-M 8/175; Bruun-1601 (illustrated 
plate 6); Weight: 1.04 gms. Moneyer: Outhencar. Near Choice 
with excellent details and a pleasant bluish toning.
Whereas the obverse die, which is an unusually exact copy of Aeth-
elred’s Helmet type from 1003-1009, was also used in the produc-
tion of two different Magnus types (Hauberg 8 and 9), the reverse 
die is known from combinations with no less than five different 
obverse dies in the name of Harthacnut. 
€1,000-€1,500
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Miniature of Harthacnut in a 
14th century royal genealogy.

========== PAGE 23 ==========

 Bid Online at www.StacksBowers.com Pa ge 7
Session I • Tuesday , March 24, 2026 • 12:00  PM ET / 5:00  PM CET
Excessively Rare Penny of Immense Historical Interest
17022
DENMARK. Penny, ND (1042-47). Haithabu (Hedeby) Mint. Magnus I, “the Good” (Magnus den gode). NGC 
Unc Details—Peck Marked. Hauberg-38; Hauberg Collection-728; C. J. Becker, NNUM, 1983, p. 42 ff.; Bruun-1631 
(illustrated Plate 6); Weight: 0.61 gms. Moneyer: Iule. Wonderfully toned and detailed with the peckmarks having not 
the slightest negative influence on the eye-appeal.
Whereas the names of both the moneyer and the mint are clearly spelled out (IOLI ON EITHEBI) which is in itself 
quite extraordinary for this mint, it is particularly the obverse legend that deserves attention. Here, Magnus, who was 
the son of the famous Norwegian king Olaf Haraldsson (Saint Olaf), apparently and rather provocatively decided to 
put his Norwegian Royal title: MAHNVS REX NAR (NAR is a common early medieval abbreviation of NORVEGIAE).
Given the immense historical importance of this issue, it should come as no surprise that it became the second most expen-
sive of 2,921 pre-1500 Danish coins in the 1929 Hauberg auction, only surpassed by a Double Penny from Harthacnut.
€7,000-€9,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

========== PAGE 24 ==========

Page 8 Stack’s Bowers Galleries
The L.  E. Bruun Co llection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part IV

unusually high artistic merit.

Photo Enlarged 2x
17024
DENMARK. Penny, ND (1047-75). Lund Mint. Svend II Es -
tridsen. NGC MS-62. Hauberg-28; Hauberg Collection-992; 
Bruun-1845. Weight: 1.18 gms. Moneyer: Ulfcil. Highly detailed, 
near Choice and with a perfectly adorable iridescent toning.
This beautiful Byzantine type was struck in the later part of Svend’s 
reign after he had changed his name on the coins from the Nordic 
“Svein” to the Latin, Magnus probably to underline his Christian 
faith. Interestingly, in this scarce variant an E-punch has been used 
lying down has been used as the first letter in Magnus instead of a 
regular M punch. 
€1,000-€1,200
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

from the last years of Svend’s reign bearing a reverse legend writ-

17026
DENMARK. Penny, ND (1047-75). Roskilde Mint. Svend II 
Estridsen. NGC MS-63. Hauberg-36; Hauberg Collection-1078; 
Bruun-1884; Weight: 1.09 gms. Wonderfully struck and hand -
somely toned Choice Penny from the royal residential city of 
Roskilde.
The round “dot” on the upper arm of the cross has traditionally 
been described as a die flaw but large die studies of the emissions 
in Roskilde have established that it should be considered some sort 
of control mark (Fornitz, 1995). 
€1,000-€1,500
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

The small cross in front of the portrait wasn’t added until rather 
late in the production making this particular variant very rare.

considered a religious contraction for the Divine Trinity.

standing figure carrying a crook is nimbate and could be inter -
preted as an anonymous canonized bishop but might just as well 
be an interpretation of Christ as “the good sheperd” (John 10:1-21).

 Ex: Christian Jürgensen Thomsen Collection (Copenhagen - 1876) Lot 
# 10288.
CHRISTIAN JURGENSEN THOMSEN  
(1788-1865)  
Christian Jurgensen Thomsen (1788-1865) was an internation -
ally famous antiquarian, numismatist and director of the Nation-
al Museum of Denmark. He is considered the originator of the 
three-age system of European antiquity, dividing pre-history into 
the Stone, Bronze and Iron Ages. Thomsen was also a prominent 
numismatist, and in many ways his collection of European medi-
eval coins has never been surpassed.


========== PAGE 26 ==========

Page 10 Stack’s Bowers Galleries
The L.  E. Bruun Co llection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part IV
Rare and Choice Penny from a Canonized King
17031
DENMARK. Penny, ND (1080-86). Slagelse Mint. Canute IV (Knud d. Hellige). NGC MS-63. Hauberg-9; Hauberg 
Collection - (cf.1253); Ramus-36; Poulsen-SJ1.2d; Bruun-2038. Weight: 0.90 gms. Moneyer: Atsur. A strikingly lustrous 
and decidedly Choice piece of the amusing type with the king shown ready for battle.
As the first Dane to be canonized by the Pope, king Canute received the honor on April 19, 1100. His canonization by Pope 
Paschalis II followed his 1086 assassination by rebels in St. Alban’s Priory in Odense. 
€6,000-€8,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
    
  
Photo Enlarged 2x
17032
DENMARK. Penny, ND (1103-34). Roskilde Mint. Niels. NGC MS-65. Hauberg-9; Hauberg Collection -; Poulsen-SJ9; Bruun-2153. 
Weight: 0.71 gms. Moneyer: Lon. A perfectly outstanding Gem mintstate with a vibrant luster underneath a delicate toning with hues 
of blue and yellow.
Very interesting architectural type inspired by the German Denarii and struck by a rare moneyer in the later reign of king Niels. 
€2,500-€3,500
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

========== PAGE 27 ==========

 Bid Online at www.StacksBowers.com P age 11
Session I • Tuesday , March 24, 2026 • 12:00  PM ET / 5:00  PM CET
    
  
Photo Enlarged 2x
17033
DENMARK. Penny, ND (1182-1202). Lund Mint. Canute VI. 
NGC AU-53. Hauberg-1; Hauberg Collection-1526ff; Poulsen-
SK22; Bruun-2329. Weight: 0.86 gms. A very conservatively 
graded “Valdemarian” Penny in a wonderful condition towering 
above what you could reasonably expect.
The king is shown looking over some sort of fortified, brick wall, 
whereas the legendary Archbishop, Absalon, the “founder” of Co-
penhagen” is depicted on the reverse in an imposing church portal. 
€1,800-€2,200
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
    
17034
DENMARK. Silver Gulden, 1523. Malmø Mint. Christian 
II. NGC EF-40. Dav-8220; Galster-38; Sieg-9; Schou-5; Bru -
un-3934. Weight: 28.74 gms. Mintmaster: Jørgen Kock. A most 
handsome piece with excellent details and a fine iridescent ton-
ing. Rare variant with the king’s name misspelled: CHRISTER-
NUS.
Jørgen Kock was appointed mayor of Malmö in 1523 partly for 
having assisted king Christian in financing his wars against Swe-
den by issuing debased and clipped coins. The present type is, how-
ever, struck in fine silver, presumably for the king’s personal use 
during his travels in the Netherlands and Germany and to remu-
nerate foreign mercenary soldiers. .The design was copied from 
the Nobles of king Hans but with a new reverse legend taken 
from the Liber Sapientiae 9:7: XPS IHS ELEGIT ME REGEM 
POPVLO SVO (“Christ Jesus chose me to govern his people”) 
€5,000-€7,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
    
17035
DENMARK. 4 Skilling, ND (1532). Malmø Mint. Frederik 
I. NGC MS-61. Galster-60; Sieg-26; Schou-11; Bruun-4090; 
Weight: 3.72 gms. Mintmaster: Jørgen Kock. An outstanding 
mintstate exemplar nicely struck on a perfect flan of sound metal.
Scholars and collectors have argued about the denomination of this 
coin for centuries. Sieg and Galster call it an 8 Skilling, while Schou 
(and therefore Bruun) call it a 4 Skilling. The most recent study, by 
Jensen & Skjoldager, again leans towards 4 Skilling. 
€1,000-€1,200
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
    
17036
DENMARK. Gotland. Skilling, 1524. Visby Mint. Christian 
II. NGC MS-62. Galster-143; Sieg-12; Llt-2a; Delzanno-77; 
Schou-3; Bruun-4112; Weight: 4.38 gms. Mintmaster: Hans Sey-
er. Almost Choice exemplar with most of the original silvering 
intact. Rare siege type struck for the admiral and governor of 
Gotland, Søren Norby during the war between Christian II and 
his uncle, Frederik I. Norby’s use of the Danish Coat of Arms with 
the three lions leaves no doubt that he saw the island of Gotland as 
an integrated part of Denmark. 
€2,500-€3,500
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
    
17037
DENMARK. Mark, 1544. Copenhagen Mint. Christian III. 
NGC AU-53. Hede-3B; Sieg-37.2; Schou-1; Bruun-4288. Weight: 
9.31 gms. Mintmaster: Poul Fechtel. An attractive blue ton -
ing adorns this nicely struck, scarce issue bearing the first date 
struck after the Marks were debased by c. 6 %. 
€2,500-€3,500
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).

========== PAGE 28 ==========

Page 12 Stack’s Bowers Galleries
The L.  E. Bruun Co llection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part IV
    
  
Photo Enlarged 2x
17038
DENMARK. Gold Gulden (Ungersk Gylden) Klippe, 1563. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik II. NGC MS-61. Fr-21; Hede-1; Sieg-27; 
Schou-1; Bruun-4420. Weight: 3.46 gms. Mintmaster: Johan of Vilna (Hans de Willers). A lovely Mint State Klippe, with fine details 
for issue. Flan lamination.
This klippe was struck in a number of 15,504 specimens to finance expenses in connection with the sudden outbreak of the Northern Seven 
Years' War the same year. They where rather crudely minted on unevenly cut flans and it is extremely rare to find an example this nicely 
struck.
€5,000-€7,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Joh. G. Guildal Collection (Private Sale - 1918); later published by the Danish Numismatic Society as item 375 in his collection.
    
  
Photo Enlarged 2x
17039
DENMARK. Hvid, 1582. Frederiksborg Mint. Frederik II. NGC MS-63. Hede-29; Sieg-2; Schou-26; Bruun-4547. Mintmaster: Poul 
Gulden. Engraver: Christopher Angerer. An utterly charming and distinctly Choice piece of this little minor that was minted at the 
newly opened Mint in the Frederiksborg Deer Garden northwest of Copenhagen. This was the first time roller dies were used in Den-
mark. 
€700-€900
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
    
  
Photo Enlarged 2x
17040
DENMARK. 2 Skilling, 1583. Frederiksborg Mint. Frederik II. NGC MS-64. Hede-27; Sieg-8; Schou-17; Bruun-4558. Mintmaster: 
Poul Gulden. Engraver: Christopher Angerer. A wonderfully lustrous and close to Gem piece which slight curvature clearly indicates 
that it was minted with roller dies. 
€1,200-€1,500
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

========== PAGE 29 ==========

 Bid Online at www.StacksBowers.com P age 13
Session I • Tuesday , March 24, 2026 • 12:00  PM ET / 5:00  PM CET
The Bille-Brahe family, one of the most important noble families in the his -
tory of Denmark, can be traced to the Middle Ages. The family name arose 
from a merger of the two noble families Bille and Brahe. There have been 
many prominent members of the family over the years, but the most famous 
by far is world-renowned astronomer Tycho Brahe (1546-1601), son of Otto 
Brahe and Beate Clausdatter Bille. 
The most significant collector in the Bille-Brahe family was Count Preben 
Bille-Brahe (1773-1857), who is seen here with his second wife, Johanne Car-
oline Wilhelmine b. Falbe.
They were portrayed in 1817 by C.W . Eckersberg, one of Denmark’s most tal-
ented painters from the time art historians refer to as Denmark’s golden age. It 
shows a tanned man who was out on the farm during the day and in the eve-
ning sat with a book in his hand, together with his spouse, who, of course, was 
occupied with her needlework. The painting can be seen at the Ny Carlsberg 
Glyptotek in Copenhagen.
As a young man, Preben Bille-Brahe trained as an officer in the horse guard, 
after which (as the times dictated), he set off on a Grand Tour, which led him 
to Italy. It was there his great interest in art and history, so exquisitely reflected in his coin collection, was truly awak-
ened. He also visited the British Isles to study agriculture to benefit the agricultural exploitation of the family estate, 
Hvedholm. In 1798, the Countship of Brahesminde was established by uniting the lands from Hvedholm, Damsbo, 
Stensgård and Østrupgård as well as the two islands Avernakø and Drejø. Preben Bille-Brahe was considered both a 
skilled and humane estate administrator, particularly active in improving the living conditions of small-scale work-
ers, village teachers, etc. He not only had humanistic interests, but was also generally interested in science; it is by no 
means accidental that Eckersberg let Bille-Brahe hold a book in his hand. The count’s personal library at Hvedholm 
was eagerly added to, but he also supported the creation of a public library on Funen. In 1840 he received the Grand 
Cross of the Order of Dannebrog and in 1841 he was appointed Privy Councilor to the court. 
His son, diplomat Henrik Bille-Brahe (1798-1875) and grandson, Preben Charles Bille-Brahe Selby (1842-1918) 
continued to increase the collection. But at the disentailing of the estate in 1920, the countship’s collection held in 
Hvedholm Manor had to be disposed of in favor of the Treasury. The collection was valued at DKK 175,000 by Julius 
W . Wilcke and H. H. Schou, who prepared the main 
part of the L. E. Bruun catalog. With the intervention 
of Georg Galster (who was head of the coin cabinet in 
the National Museum, to which L. E. Bruun’s collection 
would be bequeathed in the unlikely event that the col-
lection held here should be destroyed or stolen within 
the next 100 years), it was decided to accept Bruun’s 
bid of DKK 140,000, and the deal was completed on 21 
October, 1922. 
Many of the most sublime pieces in L. E. Bruun’s collec-
tion come from the collection of the Countship of Bra-
hesminde, and there is no doubt that many specimens 
among the 19th century coins went straight from the 
Mint into this collection. The Bille-Brahe family’s close 
relationship with other Danish noble families, where 
exceptional pieces had been treasured for generations, 
enabled them to acquire coins that would otherwise 
not have been available to collectors. 
THE COLLECTION OF
THE COUNTSHIP OF BRAHESMINDE
Count Preben Bille-Brahe with his second 
wife, Johanne Caroline Wilhelmine b. Falbe.
Aerial view of Hvedholm Castle.

========== PAGE 30 ==========

Page 14 Stack’s Bowers Galleries
The L.  E. Bruun Co llection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part IV
Imposing and Important Half Portugaloser
Only Three Examples Recorded
17041
DENMARK. 1/2 Portugaloser, 1592. Haderslev Mint. Christian IV . NGC AU Details—Plugged, Tooled. Fr-63; 
Hede-6; Sieg-160; Harck-2.7; Schou-6; Bruun-4597. Weight: 17.38 gms. Mintmaster: Andreas Metzner. As almost all 
known examples from the Haderslev series of 1/4, 1/2 and 1 Portugalosers, the present piece has been mounted to be 
worn, evidenced by a plug above the arms. Attractively detailed and with a unusually even planchet. Only three pieces 
are known of this internationally coveted type: this, the one in the Danish National Museum and another example in 
private ownership that has also been mounted.
With a sales price of 41 Rigsdaler, this piece, that was bought by count Henrik Bille-Brahe, was one of the absolutely 
most expensive pieces in the 1867 auction over the famous Danish numismatist, Ole Devegge’s formidable collection.
This numismatically and historically highly important gold type was struck on the orders of the dowager queen Sophia on 
behalf of her son, who was only 15 at the time and had not yet been crowned. The design is a relatively close copy of the 
original Portuguese gold coins that gave their name to this popular trade coin. Following a complaint by the Council of State 
the central heraldic field differs from the otherwise basically similar first series (1591-1592) in having the two bars of Olden-
burg and the cross of Delmenhorst in the center of the coat of arms instead of the horseman of Ditmarschen (Ditmarsken).
€90,000-€110,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).
Ex: Ole Devegge Collection (Copenhagen – 1867) Lot # 428.

========== PAGE 31 ==========

 Bid Online at www.StacksBowers.com P age 15
Session I • Tuesday , March 24, 2026 • 12:00  PM ET / 5:00  PM CET
Magnificently Detailed and Historically Interesting
Best of Only Two in Private Ownership
17042
DENMARK. 1/4 Portugaloser, 1592. Haderslev Mint. Christian IV . NGC AU Details—Mount Removed. Fr-64; 
Hede-3; Sieg-155; Harck-2.4; Schou-7; Bruun-4598. Weight: 8.69 gms. Mintmaster: Andreas Metzner. Remains from 
a contemporary mounting positioned right above the royal coat of arms presumably to be used as a medallion by high 
ranking dignitaries. Magnificently detailed with even the profile of the man in the moon clearly visible on the mintmark.
Of the handful of known exemplars of this important one-year type, only one other is in private hands. This was ac-
quired by L. E. Bruun with his purchase of the Johan G. Guildal collection in 1918. As the Bille-Brahe exemplar however 
was in much better overall condition, the Guildal piece was sold in the 1925 auction of duplicates (#1748).
Rather unconventionally; the central field of the coat of arms is occupied by the horseman of Ditmarschen (Ditmarsken) 
instead of the two bars of Oldenburg and the cross of Delmenhorst. Following a complaint by the Council of State this was 
however changed for the subsequent issues.
€45,000-€65,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).

========== PAGE 32 ==========

Page 16 Stack’s Bowers Galleries
The L.  E. Bruun Co llection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part IV
    
17043
DENMARK. 2 Skilling, 1595. Copenhagen Mint. Christian IV . NGC MS-61. Hede-67; Sieg-28; Harck-3.1.2; Schou-12; Bruun-4613. 
Moneyer: Andreas Metzner. A truly exceptional survivor of this scarce type bearing the legend REX ELECTVS (King-elect) and no 
crown above the shield indicating that it was struck before the actual coronation on the 29th of August 1596 when the king had come 
of age. 
€800-€1,200
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
    
17044
DENMARK. 4 Skilling, 1596. Copenhagen Mint. Christian IV . NGC MS-62. Hede-71; Sieg-38; Schou-8; Harck-4.4; Bruun-4627. 
Mintmaster: Gert van Campen. An absolutely stunning near Choice example of this rare and interesting one year type which was 
struck in connection with the coronation of the king on 29 August, possibly as a largesse money. 
€1,500-€2,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
    
17045
DENMARK. 8 Solidi, 1603. Copenhagen Mint. Christian IV . NGC MS-62. KM-15; Hede-74; Sieg-52; Schou-9; Aagaard-4; 
Harck-6.23.1; Bruun-4652. Weight: 2.98 gms. Mintmaster: Nicolaus Scwabe. Engraver: Nicolaus Schwabe. A superb, dark and multi-
colored patina further enhances the allure of this already irresistible near Choice exemplar.
This beautiful and amusing type was according to tradition struck as largesse money to be used in connection with the celebration of the 
Hanseatic city of Hamburg swearing allegiance to the Danish king on 30 October 1603. 
€4,000-€5,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Christian IV by 
Pieter Isaacsz (1569–1625)

========== PAGE 33 ==========

 Bid Online at www.StacksBowers.com P age 17
Session I • Tuesday , March 24, 2026 • 12:00  PM ET / 5:00  PM CET
Very Rare and Unusual Gold Klippe
17046
DENMARK. 4 Daler Klippe, 1604. Copenhagen Mint. Christian IV . NGC AU Details--Edge Filing. Fr-46; KM-25; 
Hede-12A; Sieg-146; Harck-6.4.2; Schou-7; Bruun-4671. Weight: 9.74 gms. Mintmaster: Nicolaus Schwabe. Unusually 
nicely struck for type and with remains of original luster. Two different obverse dies were used with markedly different 
portraits of which this, resembling the one used for minor coins like the Soesling, Hede-82, is by far the rarest with only 
a very few in private ownership.
Schwabe, who had immigrated from Saxony, was assigned to the Royal Danish court in 1595 and became the first to pro-
duce struck medals in Denmark. In 1602 he was appointed mintmaster in Copenhagen where this wonderful gold klippe 
was struck.
€15,000-€18,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).
    
17047
DENMARK. 4 Skilling, 1604. Copenhagen Mint. Christian 
IV . NGC MS-63. KM-11; Hede-78A; Sieg-39.1; Harck-6.29.1; 
Schou-24; Bruun-4682. Mintmaster: Nicolaus Schwabe. An alto-
gether outstanding piece of this elusive minor. 
€700-€900
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
    
17048
DENMARK. 4 Skilling, 1606. Copenhagen Mint. Christian 
IV . NGC EF-40. KM-30; Hede-94; Sieg-41.1; Harck-6.34.1; 
Schou-19; Bruun-4718. Mintmaster: Nicolaus Schwabe. An ex -
tremely rare date with a lovely old cabinet toning. 
€2,000-€3,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

========== PAGE 34 ==========

Page 18 Stack’s Bowers Galleries
The L.  E. Bruun Co llection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part IV
Attractive and Amusing Elephant Rarity
17049
DENMARK. Rose Noble, 1611. Copenhagen Mint. Christian IV . NGC AU-55. Fr-48; KM-51; Hede-23A; Sieg-143.1; 
Harck-8.4.2; Schou-2; Bruun-4841. Weight: 8.69 gms. Mintmaster: Nicolaus Schwabe. Considerable original luster and 
fine details combine in making this a very attractive piece. Unusual variant where the armored right arm of the king 
divides the legend.
The inspiration for this beautiful, amusing and very rare type came from the British Rose Nobles that were widely used 
in international trade. In the contemporary, official accounts the type is referred to as a “Guldridder” from the Dutch, 
“Gouden rijder”.
€70,000-€90,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).
    
17050
DENMARK. 4 Skilling, 1617. Copenhagen Mint. Christian IV . NGC MS-65. KM-55.2; Hede-100B; Sieg-43.2; Harck-11.2.3; 
Schou-28; Bruun-4996. Mintmaster: Johan Post. One of the key variants of the type in a spectacular, absolutely pristine and delicately 
toned Gem condition. 
€1,000-€1,200
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

17051
DENMARK. 4 Kroneskilling, 1620. Copenhagen Mint. Christian IV . NGC MS-63. KM-81; Hede-115A; Sieg-45.1; Harck-18.2.1. 
Schou-143; Bruun-5181. Mintmaster: Nicolaus Schwabe. Remarkably well-preserved example and the highest graded by NGC for 
both date and type. A flat and even planchet with no bends or creases, fairly well-centered and with a breathtaking blue toning over 
the uncirculated surfaces. 
€800-€1,000

Extremely Rare One-Y ear Type with Bird Mintmark
17052
DENMARK. 2 Gold Krone, 1621. Copenhagen Mint. Christian IV . NGC AU Details—Obverse Scratched. Fr-54; 
KM-74.1; Hede-25C; Sieg-134.3; Schou-1; Harck-15.1.2; Bruun-5272. Weight: 5.89 gms. Mintmaster: Johan Post. Split 
in flan, but overall a very pleasing exemplar. Of this one-year type where Johan Post uses a charming and elegantly ren-
dered bird mintmark only 2 privately owned pieces have been recorded by Anders Harck.
€8,000-€10,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

========== PAGE 36 ==========

Page 20 Stack’s Bowers Galleries
The L.  E. Bruun Co llection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part IV
Unique and Highly Interesting Klippe
Deliciously Toned and Exceptionally Detailed
    
17053
DENMARK. 2 Speciedaler Klippe, 1623. Glückstadt Mint. Christian IV . NGC AU Details--Edge Filing. Dav-unlist-
ed (cf. 3667); KM-Pn3; Hede-159B; Sieg-unlisted (cf. 184); Harck-22.2; Schou-10; Lange - (cf. 32); Bruun-5328. Weight: 
57.13 gms. Mintmaster: Albert Dionis. Unique and highly interesting trial strike minted on a square planchet. Perfectly 
centered, deliciously toned and showing exceptional details seldom visible on the regular issues. The noted edge filing 
should be considered part of the minting process.
A total of 6 trial strikes using Speciedaler dies are known, all of which are unique as weights and dimensions varies con-
siderably. 3 are struck on a square flan, 2 on a hexagonal flan and one on a octagonal flan. Only three have the weight of 
a Double Specie, of which this is the only one in private hands while the two others are in the Danish National Museum.
The city of Glückstadt (literally: City of luck) had been founded by Christian IV only seven years earlier, in 1616. The idea 
was to creative a Danish rival to the important City of Hamburg. Promising settlers tax exemption and, radically for the 
time, freedom of religion, the city soon flourished, becoming the home of the first Jewish settlers in Denmark. Many of the 
coins struck at Glückstadt depict Lady Luck standing on a globe, as does this astounding piece.
€50,000-€60,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Joh. G. Guildal Collection (Private Sale - 1918); later published by the Danish Numismatic Society as item 723 in his collection 
and illustrated page 209.

========== PAGE 37 ==========

 Bid Online at www.StacksBowers.com P age 21
Session I • Tuesday , March 24, 2026 • 12:00  PM ET / 5:00  PM CET
Very Rare and Majestic Triple Speciedaler
17054
DENMARK. 3 Speciedaler, 1624. Copenhagen Mint. Christian IV . NGC AU Details—Cleaned. Dav-3522; KM-75; 
Hede-57; Sieg-109; Harck-23.4.1; Schou-3; Bruun-5365. Weight: 86.94 gms. Mintmaster: Nicolaus Schwabe (NS). A 
formidable exemplar of this very rare and majestic piece. 
In an entry of March 31st the king wrote in one of his diaries that he had received 140 Speciedalers from Schwabe which 
he had struck from Norwegian silver. Even though there might have been silver from other sources in the alloy there can be 
little doubt that the beautiful 1624-series is the first of the Danish/Norwegian coins to be struck from silver actually mined 
at Kongsberg Silver Works which was founded shortly after finding silver ores in the cliffs in the summer of 1623.
€16,000-€20,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Joh. G. Guildal Collection (Private Sale - 1918); later published by the Danish Numismatic Society as item 744 in his collection.
Ex: Alfred Benzon Collection (Copenhagen - 1887) Lot # 554.
    
  
Photo Enlarged 2x
17055
DENMARK. Skilling, 1624. Copenhagen Mint. Christian IV . NGC MS-64. KM-88; Hede-135; Sieg-25.2; Harck-23.19.2; Schou-147; 
Bruun-5432. Mintmaster: Nicolaus Schwabe. Closed crown variation. A most surprising near-Gem survivor of this dainty one year 
type. 
€600-€800
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

17056
DENMARK. 2 Gold Krone, 1628. Copenhagen Mint. Christian IV . NGC AU Details—Edge Filing. Fr-54; KM-74.2; 
Hede-25A; Sieg-134.1; Schou-6; Harck-25.2; Bruun-5543. Weight: 5.95 gms. Mintmaster: Nicolaus Schwabe. Excep -
tionally well-struck on a sound planchet. Very rare.
€10,000-€12,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

17057
DENMARK. 4 Skilling, 1645. Copenhagen Mint. Christian IV . NGC MS-64. KM-133.1; H-150; Sieg-47.2; Schou-86; Harck-35.10.2; 
Aagaard-K9; Bruun-5747. Mintmaster: Heinrich Köhler. An immensely attractive minor from the ever popular “Hebrew-series” that 
practically never turn up in really excellent condition like the present near-Gem piece. 
€2,000-€3,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

========== PAGE 39 ==========

 Bid Online at www.StacksBowers.com P age 23
Session I • Tuesday , March 24, 2026 • 12:00  PM ET / 5:00  PM CET
    
17058
DENMARK. Speciedaler, 1646/5. Glückstadt Mint. Christian IV . NGC MS-60. Dav-3670; KM-27; Hede-163C; Sieg-186.3; 
Harck-38.4.3; Schou-35; Lange-40b; Bruun-5801. Weight: 28.75 gms. Mintmaster: Jacob Schwieger (IS). Engraver: signed (IL). A most 
pleasing piece of this ever popular type that is rarely encountered in MS condition.
The nude Roman goddess of fortune and personification of luck, Fortuna, standing on a globe and waving a banner, was chosen by Chris-
tian IV as a metaphor when the city of Glückstadt was founded in 1616. 
€2,500-€3,500
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Joh. G. Guildal Collection (Private Sale - 1918); later published by the Danish Numismatic Society as item 1041 in his collection.
    
17059
DENMARK. Ducat, 1647. Copenhagen Mint. Christian IV . NGC MS-62. Fr-39; KM-141; Hede-33; Sieg-128.2; Harck-35.3.3; 
Schou-1; Aagaard-KG5; Bruun-5812. Weight: 3.48 gms. Mintmaster: Heinrich Köhler. Engraver: Hans zum Busch. Struck with a new 
obverse die and a reengraved die from 1646. With a nice, even flan, excellent details and plenty of luster, this exemplar markedly excels 
from other Ducats of this interesting type.
In the middle of the Torstenson War and possibly to invoke the help of God against the Swedes, king Christian personally sketched the coin 
design using the Tetragrammaton (the Hebrew four-letter-name of God) as a key element on silver and gold coins struck in Copenhagen, 
Christiania, and Glückstadt. 
€7,000-€9,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).
Ex: Peter Frederik Suhm Collection (Copenhagen - 1802) Lot # 62.

========== PAGE 40 ==========

Page 24 Stack’s Bowers Galleries
The L.  E. Bruun Co llection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part IV
BARON HENRIK HIELMSTIERNE 
Baron Henrik Hielmstierne (1715-1780) was a prominent Danish civil servant 
and historian who became one of Scandinavia’s most significant collectors dur-
ing the Enlightenment. Born Henrik Henrichsen to a wealthy Icelandic mer -
chant in Copenhagen, he quickly distinguished himself through his academic 
talent, leading to a prestigious career as a jurist and state official. His contribu-
tions to society were formally recognized in 1747 when he was ennobled under 
the name Hielmstierne.
As a member of The Royal Society of London and other elite scientific circles, 
Hielmstierne applied a rigorous, systematic approach to his private passions. 
He amassed a staggering collection of over 10,000 books, including rare incu -
nabula, alongside an extensive array of coins, medals, paintings, and prints. His 
expertise was so profound that he even assisted in cataloging the Danish Kings' 
own coin collections.
Hielmstierne’s ultimate legacy lies in how his private treasures helped shape 
Denmark's national identity. Following his death, his daughter, Agnete Marie 
(1752 – 1838), ensured that his life's work benefited the public. She donated 
his vast library to the Royal Danish Library and his art collection to the royal 
collections at what is now the National Gallery of Denmark. In 1812, his renowned coin collection was sold on very 
favorable terms to King Frederik VI for the Royal Collection of Coins and Medals. While much of the coin collection 
was later sold to prominent collectors like C.J. Thomsen and Preben Bille-Brahe due to the economic crises follow-
ing the Napoleonic Wars, Hielmstierne’s systematic rigor in preserving cultural heritage remains a cornerstone of 
Danish history.
Portrait of 
Henrik Hielmstierne by Jens Juel

========== PAGE 41 ==========

 Bid Online at www.StacksBowers.com P age 25
Session I • Tuesday , March 24, 2026 • 12:00  PM ET / 5:00  PM CET
Spectacular Type with an Amazing Pedigree
Probably Unique in Private Ownership
17060
DENMARK. 1/4 Ducat, ND (1646-48). Copenhagen Mint. Christian IV . NGC MS-61. Fr-60; KM-150; Hede-41; 
Sieg-123; Harck-36.6; Schou-14; Bruun-5843. Weight: 0.87 gms. Mintmaster: Heinrich Köhler. Engraver: Hans zum 
Busch. A wonderful and perfectly centered exemplar with a marvelous pedigree. Only three examples have been re -
corded of this amazing coin: this, one in The Danish National Museum, and one in the Coin and Medal Collection of 
Odense Museum.
Due to its “spectacular” design this type has always ranked among the most coveted of all from the Danish Renaissance. The 
latin inscription, VIDE MIRA DOMI (“Behold the wonders of the Lord”) found below a pair of spectacles refers to the gold 
that was found near Arendal in Norway.
The king had high hopes for this find and had this highly unusual series of Ducats made from the Norwegian gold which 
was brought to Copenhagen to be refined by the alchemist and goldsmith, Caspar Herbach.
€30,000-€40,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).
Ex: The Royal Danish Collection of Coins and Medals.
Ex: Henrik Hielmstierne (Collection Catalog, Copenhagen - 1786) # 24.

========== PAGE 42 ==========

Page 26 Stack’s Bowers Galleries
The L.  E. Bruun Co llection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part IV
Awe-Inspiring and Wonderfully Detailed Quadruple Taler
Largest of All Danish Broad Talers in Private Ownership
17061
DENMARK. 4 Speciedaler, ND (1607-1610). Elsinore Mint (Helsingør). Christian IV . NGC AU Details—Graffiti. 
Dav-3537D; KM-115; Hede-63B; Sieg-115; Harck-7.4; Schou-16; Bruun-5844. Weight: 115.62 gms. Mintmaster: Hans 
Flemming (HF). This awe-inspiring piece is the largest of all Danish Broad Talers in private ownership. What seems to 
be the only other Quadruple Speciedaler of this type rests in the Danish National Museum. Sharply struck with wonder-
ful details, luster and a nice, discreet cabinet toning.
This stunning presentation coin, which was clearly inspired by the German Lösers, was struck in the city of Shakespeare’s 
Hamlet, by the Dutchman, Hans Flemming. Rather unusually, the king is shown wearing a feathered hat, but the two sol-
diers behind and underneath the horse set a military scene.
€30,000-€40,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Antoine Feill Collection (Frankfurt – 1907) Lot # 16.

========== PAGE 43 ==========

 Bid Online at www.StacksBowers.com P age 27
Session I • Tuesday , March 24, 2026 • 12:00  PM ET / 5:00  PM CET
    
17062
DENMARK. 2 Speciedaler, ND (1611). Copenhagen Mint. Christian IV . NGC AU Details—Cleaned. Dav-LS533; KM-154; Hede-
64B; Sieg-119.1; Harck- 6.13.1; Schou-22; Bruun-5847. Weight: 57.58 gms. Mintmaster: Nicolaus Schwabe. Engraver: Isaac Henniges 
(IH). Pleasantly re-toning from a old, very gentle cleaning, still retaining original luster and with spectacular details.
On this majestic presentation piece the king is clad in armor and carries a marshal’s baton as a symbol of his role as the supreme com-
mander in the successful Kalmar War against Sweden. These so-called “breddalere” (“Broad Talers”) became so popular that early casts 
were made to supply the demand, to be used in the lid of tankards, for instance. Original strikes in nice condition are rare. 
€7,000-€9,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Johan Andreas Ferdinand Wolff Collection (Copenhagen – 1882) Lot # 672.
    
17063
DENMARK. Ducat Klippe, 1648. Copenhagen Mint. Freder -
ik III. NGC AU-55. Fr-75; KM-163.1; Hede-5B; Sieg-127; 
Schou-28; Aagaard-6.1 (48-2/48-2); Bruun-5983. Weight: 3.45 
gms. Mintmaster: Henrik Köhler. Engraver: Hans zum Busch. 
Rare.
For the celebration of the king's coronation on the 23rd of Novem-
ber a series of unusual coins were minted on square flans to be used 
as largesse money. Silver coins with a total value of 1000 Rigsdalers 
were minted in denominations of 1/4, 1/6 and 1/12 Speciedaler to 
be thrown to the crowd in the streets of Copenhagen. It has been 
speculated that the reason for making the coin square was so they 
would not roll down the open sewers. Gold coins were also minted 
as Ducats and 1/2 Ducats that might have been used in the same 
way. The present piece with a small head of the king was struck 
from dies that were also used for issuing 1/12 Speciedalers.
The reverse has been interpreted as either an urn spewing ashes or 
as an incense burner. On the receptacle is written, TANDEM, the 
Latin word for "finally" which might refer to the fact that Christian 
IV , after nearly 60 years on the throne, had finally gone to meet his 
maker.
€5,000-€7,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).
    
17064
DENMARK. 1/4 Speciedaler Klippe, 1648. Copenhagen 
Mint. Frederik III. NGC MS-65. KM-161; Hede-47; Sieg-67; 
Aagaard-67.1; Schou-33; Bruun-5987. Weight: 7.11 gms. Mint -
master: Heinrich Köhler. Engraver: Hans zum Busch. An out of 
this world exemplar of this fascinating largesse Klippe. Generous 
hues of blue and gold adorns the superlatively lustrous fields of 
this Gem MS-65 equally destined for a choice collection of Dan-
ish type coins and an illustrious collection of European Klippes.
€5,000-€7,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

========== PAGE 44 ==========

Page 28 Stack’s Bowers Galleries
The L.  E. Bruun Co llection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part IV
Stunning Roman Type Mint State Ducat
17065
DENMARK. Ducat, 1651. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik III. NGC MS-62. Fr-77; KM-171; Hede-7; Sieg-109; Schou-1; 
Aagaard-8.3; Bruun-6028. Weight: 3.45 gms. Mintmaster: Henrik Köhler. A stunning example with bright luster and 
excellent details.
The obverse shows Frederik III dressed as a Roman emperor wearing toga and a laurel wreath, while the king’s royal 
motto: DOMINVS PROVIDEBIT (“The Lord will provide”) takes an uncommonly prominent place on the reverse. It clearly 
emerges from mintmaster Heinrich Köhler’s accounts that this early Ducat wasn’t minted at the request of the king but for 
the use of wealthy private citizens.
€7,000-€10,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

========== PAGE 45 ==========

 Bid Online at www.StacksBowers.com P age 29
Session I • Tuesday , March 24, 2026 • 12:00  PM ET / 5:00  PM CET
Luminous and Monumental Half Portugaloser
Best of Only Two in Private Hands
    
17066
DENMARK. 5 Ducat (1/2 Portugaloser), 1653. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik III. NGC MS-62. Fr-106; KM-Pn10; 
Hede-52A; Sieg-71.1 (silver); Schou-3; Aagaard-81.1; Bruun-6082. Weight: 17.31 gms. Mintmaster: Henrik Köhler 
(HK). Engraver: Jeremias Hercules. A luminous and positively awe-inspiring example of a monumental and highly 
important gold type presumably struck for the personal use of the king. Of the utmost rarity and although known from 
public collections in Copenhagen, Odense, Vienna, St. Petersburg and Washington, this is the best of what appears to 
be only two in private hands.
€100,000-€130,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Alfred Benzon Collection (Copenhagen - 1887) Lot # 987.
Ex: L. Christian Nielsen Collection (Copenhagen - 1878) Lot # 35.

========== PAGE 46 ==========

Page 30 Stack’s Bowers Galleries
The L.  E. Bruun Co llection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part IV
One of Two Recorded and Most Handsome
17067
DENMARK. Ducat, 1653. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik III. NGC AU-55. Fr-unlisted; KM-197; Hede-9; Sieg-110; 
Schou-11; Aagaard-10.1; Bruun-6088. Weight: 3.46 gms. Mintmaster: Henrik Köhler. Engravers: Jeremias Hercules / 
Hans zum Busch. A very pleasant piece with a nice warm toning and some original luster. Apart from the example in 
the Danish National Museum, this is the only recorded specimen by Aagaard.
With all the previous gold types from Frederik III showing the king wearing a laurel wreath this is the first time he is 
portrayed with a crown. The reverse die was however cut in 1651 and had already been used for the laureled type (Fr-77; 
Sieg-109).
€14,000-€18,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).
    
  
Photo Enlarged 2x
17068
DENMARK. 2 Skilling, 1655. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik III. NGC MS-65. KM-A190; Hede-126B; Sieg-17.3; Schou-57; 
Aagaard-34; Bruun-6201. Mintmaster: Henrik Köhler. A full Gem with a mesmerizing, iridescent, rainbow toning. 
€500-€700
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

========== PAGE 47 ==========

 Bid Online at www.StacksBowers.com P age 31
Session I • Tuesday , March 24, 2026 • 12:00  PM ET / 5:00  PM CET
Utterly Impressive and Rare Half Portugaloser
Dramatic and Historically Engaging Reverse
17069
DENMARK. 5 Ducat (1/2 Portugaloser), 1659. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik III. NGC AU-55. Fr-124; KM-PnG16; 
Hede-98; Sieg-52; Schou-4; Aagaard-74.1 (59-E1/59-E1); Bruun-6274. Weight: 17.40 gms. Mintmaster: Henrik Köhler. 
An utterly attractive piece with plenty of original luster, a delicate golden toning and wonderful details. Struck on an 
unusually even flan.
This iconic type was struck to commemorate the failure of the Swedish king, Carl X Gustav, to capture Copenhagen. The 
monogram of King Frederik III is shown above a stone with the text EBEN EZER, referring to the Old Testament (1 Samuel 
7:12) in which Samuel erected a “stone of help” (Eben-ha-Ezer) between Mizpah and Jeshanah to honor the Lord for his help 
in securing the Israelites a victory over the Philistines.
The Latin inscription on the reverse, SOLI DEO GLORIA, is another tribute to God (“To God alone be the glory”) while 
the dramatic design shows the hand of God (Manus Dei) severing the hand of the Swedish king with the sword of justice as 
he reaches out for the Danish crown. It has been suggested that the very rare pieces struck in gold might have been used as 
distinctions given to officers in the civic guard who had distinguished themselves during the siege.
€80,000-€100,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

========== PAGE 48 ==========

Page 32 Stack’s Bowers Galleries
The L.  E. Bruun Co llection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part IV
    
17070
DENMARK. Gold Krone, 1660. Glückstadt Mint. Frederik III. NGC Unc Details—Edge Filing. Fr-120; KM-40.2; Hede-145A; 
Sieg-160A; Schou-1; Aagaard-3.2 (57.1-60.3); Bruun-6343. Weight: 5.99 gms. Mintmaster: Jacob Schwieger. Engraver: Matthias Först 
(obverse), Johan Blum (reverse). Traces of edge filing does not alter the fact that this piece when it comes to original luster, a sound flan 
and sublime details is far superior to what one would normally encounter. 
€7,000-€9,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).
Ex: Hans Henrik Frost Collection (Copenhagen - 1826) Lot # 28.
Highly Brilliant and Extremely Rare
17071
DENMARK. 2 Ducat, 1662. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik III. NGC MS-60. Fr-117; KM-244; Hede-33A; Sieg-131.1; 
Schou-6; Aagaard-28.1 (62-1/62-2); Bruun-6384. Weight: 6.92 gms. Mintmaster: Johan Stichmann (IS). Engraver: Jo-
han Blum. Highly brilliant and attractive exemplar of the extremely rare one-year type where the reverse cross divides 
the date.
This beautiful gold type clearly mimics Blum’s Taler-type from 1662. The mintmaster and goldsmith Johan Stichmann is 
known to have been in charge of a part of the militia when Copenhagen was unsuccessfully stormed by the Swedes on 11 
February 1659.
€12,000-€14,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).

========== PAGE 49 ==========

 Bid Online at www.StacksBowers.com P age 33
Session I • Tuesday , March 24, 2026 • 12:00  PM ET / 5:00  PM CET
Truly Majestic Baroque Portugaloser
Excessively Rare and Important
17072
DENMARK. 10 Ducat (Portugaloser), 1663. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik III. NGC AU Details—Obverse Dam -
age. Fr-113; KM-Unlisted; Hede-61; Sieg-79 (incorrectly giving the date 1662); Schou-1; Aagaard-97.2 (62.1/63-1); 
Bruun-6398. Weight: 34.50 gms. Mintmaster: Johan Stichmann (IS). Engraver: Johan Blum. A marvelous, excessively 
rare and truly majestic piece that would befit any advanced collections of European Baroque gold coins.
Whether or not this issue, which was minted shortly before the death of Johan Stichmann, should be considered an off-strike 
in gold of a regular Speciedaler intended for the king’s personal use is not quite clear, especially since the reverse die used is 
not known from any other examples, silver or gold. In total four 10 Ducats of this type (1662 and 1663) have been recorded 
by Aagaard.
The engraver Johan Blum (ca. 1599 - 1669) had been working as an engraver at the Mint in Bremen. In 1657 he was ap-
pointed medalist in Copenhagen by Frederik III and married a Dane. He was technically very accomplished and artistically 
his work is inspired by Sebastian Dadler.
€110,000-€130,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).

========== PAGE 50 ==========

Page 34 Stack’s Bowers Galleries
The L.  E. Bruun Co llection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part IV
Very Rare Double Taler with Lots of Eye Appeal
17073
DENMARK. 2 Speciedaler, 1663. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik III. NGC AU Details—Cleaned. Dav-3547; KM-240; 
Hede-62A; Sieg-80.1; Schou-11; Aagaard-96.2; Bruun-6403. Weight: 57.86 gms. Mintmaster: Johann Stichmann (IS). 
Engraver: Johan Blum. Nicely re-toning after an old, light cleaning, this commanding piece has lots of eye-appeal. Very 
rare.
€12,000-€15,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Joh. G. Guildal Collection (Private Sale - 1918); later published by the Danish Numismatic Society as item 1459 in his collec-
tion.

========== PAGE 51 ==========

 Bid Online at www.StacksBowers.com P age 35
Session I • Tuesday , March 24, 2026 • 12:00  PM ET / 5:00  PM CET
Dazzling Portugaloser with Superlative Details
Best of Presumably Only Two in Private Hands
17074
DENMARK. 10 Ducats (Portugaloser), 1664. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik III. NGC AU Details—Cleaned. Fr-113; 
KM-unlisted; Hede-62B; Sieg-80.2; Schou-1; Aagaard-99.1; Bruun-6410. Weight: 34.79 gms. Mintmaster: Caspar Her-
bach (CH). Engraver: Johan Blum. Although at some point gently cleaned this gorgeous 10 Ducat dazzles with abun-
dant luster and superlative details.
Bruun owned a duplicate of this coin from the Brahesminde Collection. This was offered at the 1925 sale of duplicates in 
1925 as lot 2582 and sold to Virgil M. Brand. It realized DKK 2,100 which was the second highest price of all the 6,241 
lots offered, in spite of being scuffed and mounted, underlining the extreme importance of this extraordinary issue.
A shelved die for Johan Stichmann’s Speciedaler from 1662 was used for the obverse, whereas the reverse die is known 
from gold 5 and 10 Ducats and a unique Double Taler held in the National Museum of Denmark.
Presumably struck for the king’s personal use. It is also quite likely that this particular example has in fact been in royal 
possession since it was first made. It was bequeathed to counsellor of state, Julius Gustav Berger Klemp (1813-1890), who 
had acted as executor of king Frederik VII’s estate by his widow, countess Louise Danner.
€150,000-€200,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: J. G. B. Klemp Collection (Copenhagen - 1891) Lot # 343.
Presumably ex: King Frederik VII of Denmark Collection.

Page 36 Stack’s Bowers Galleries
The L.  E. Bruun Co llection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part IV
Near Choice Maritime Double Ducat
17075
DENMARK. 2 Ducat, 1664. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik III. NGC MS-62. Fr-112; KM-216.2; Hede-28B; Sieg-133.2; 
Schou-19; Aagaard-38.1 ; Bruun-6414. Weight: 6.95 gms. Mintmaster: Caspar Herbach. Engraver: Johan Blum. A scarce 
and always popular nautical type in a near Choice condition far above what you could realistically hope to encounter.
The beautifully rendered full-rigged ship on the reverse is used to symbolize the fact that the coin itself had been struck with 
gold brought back from overseas.
€14,000-€18,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).
Ex: Peter v. Hemmert Collection (Copenhagen - 1807) Lot # 93.

========== PAGE 53 ==========

 Bid Online at www.StacksBowers.com P age 37
Session I • Tuesday , March 24, 2026 • 12:00  PM ET / 5:00  PM CET
Choice Mint State and Apparently Unique in Private Ownership
17076
DENMARK. Ducat, 1667/6. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik III. NGC MS-63. Fr-unlisted (cf. 104); KM-294.; Hede-26B; 
Sieg-124.2; Schou-7; Aagaard-52.1; Bruun-6575. Weight: 3.41 gms. Mintmaster: Frederik Caspar Herbach. Engraver: 
Jeremias Hercules. Choice Mint State and apparently unique in private ownership.
Frederik Caspar Herbach continued the design used by his two predecessors but asked Hercules to make the dies a little 
smaller and substitute the crown with a laurel wreath. The type was only minted in 1667 and is exceptionally rare. The 
present sub-type where the Copenhagen mintmark (a clover) almost blends in with the reverse legend is only known in two 
examples, this and the one in the Danish National Museum.
€20,000-€25,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).
Ex: Peter v. Hemmert Collection (Copenhagen - 1807) Lot # 105.

of blue. A very rare type with a lion’s head on the king’s armor.

17079
DENMARK. Ducat, 1668. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik III. NGC Unc Details—Obverse Damage. Fr-82; KM-304; Hede-35; Sieg-
125; Schou-3; Aagaard-61.2; Bruun-6640. Weight: 3.48 gms. Mintmaster: Gotfred Krüger (GK). Engraver: Johan Blum. A small nick 
in the obverse right field does not deter from the overall beauty of this highly lustrous Ducat. Very rare type only minted for two years. 
The date on the reverse die has been altered from 1667.
This Ducat bears close resemblance to Krüger’s Speciedalers from the same year (Hede 73), and silver off-strikes have traditionally been 
seen as 1/8 Speciedalers. 
€8,000-€10,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
